NEDA Lab
Nutritional Epidemiology · Data Analysis

Examining associations between diet, nutritional factors, and health across populations.

We study dietary exposures, eating behaviors, and body composition in relation to cardiometabolic and aging-related health.

Dietary Exposures & Eating BehaviorsObesity & Body CompositionCardiometabolic HealthEnvironmental Exposures & Healthy AgingPopulation Data & Evidence Synthesis

Welcome to the NEDA Lab

The NEDA Lab is based in the Department of Food and Nutrition at Sookmyung Women’s University. We conduct population-based and clinical research using epidemiologic and statistical approaches.

Research across diet, health, and populations.

Research

Our research program connects nutritional and environmental exposures with cardiometabolic and aging-related health, using population-based evidence to inform more personalized prevention and health management.

Research Areas

What We Study

These themes reflect the laboratory’s current research direction and Professor Jane J. Lee’s recent work in nutritional, cardiovascular, environmental, and population epidemiology.

01

Dietary Exposures & Eating Behaviors

We examine how dietary exposures and eating-related factors are associated with health outcomes across populations, including work on artificial sweeteners and evolving research in eating-related health.

Artificial SweetenersUltra-Processed FoodsDietary PatternsMeal TimingEating Behaviors
02

Obesity & Body Composition

We investigate obesity beyond conventional BMI by integrating body composition, body shape, and precision anthropometry to characterize individual differences in health risk.

ObesityBody CompositionBody Shape PhenotypingSarcopenia3D Precision Anthropometry
03

Cardiometabolic Health

We study nutritional and clinical factors related to cardiovascular and metabolic disease, with recent work spanning heart failure, diabetes, cardiovascular outcomes, mortality, and treatment-related risk.

Cardiovascular DiseaseHeart FailureDiabetesCardiometabolic OutcomesMortality
04

Environmental Exposures & Healthy Aging

We investigate how environmental exposures and age-related conditions intersect with functional health, including particulate matter exposure, sarcopenia, and cognitive function in middle-aged and older adults.

Particulate MatterSarcopeniaCognitive FunctionHealthy AgingMiddle-Aged & Older Adults
05

Population Evidence & Research Synthesis

We generate evidence from large population and clinical datasets and integrate findings across studies. Our work includes national health surveys and cohorts, systematic reviews, meta-analyses, and bibliometric research.

KNHANESKoGESNHANESClinical DataSystematic ReviewsMeta-AnalysisBibliometrics
Research Direction

From Population Evidence to Personalized Health

We move from identifying population-level patterns to characterizing individual risk and translating evidence into more targeted nutrition and health strategies.

01

Population Patterns

Identify patterns in diet, lifestyle, environmental exposures, and health outcomes across populations.

→
02

Modifiable Risk Factors

Determine nutritional, behavioral, clinical, and body-composition factors associated with disease risk.

→
03

Individual Health Profiles

Integrate body shape, clinical characteristics, and multidimensional health information to better characterize individual risk.

→
04

Personalized Prevention

Translate evidence into personalized nutrition, lifestyle intervention, and chronic disease prevention strategies.

Projects

Current research projects undertaken by the laboratory.

Ongoing Project · March 2026–February 2029

Development of a Next-Generation Personalized Nutrition and Health Management System Using AI and 3D-Based Precision Anthropometry

This project aims to develop and clinically validate a next-generation personalized nutrition and health management system that integrates AI-based analysis, three-dimensional precision anthropometry, and multidimensional health data. The system will provide personalized nutrition and lifestyle strategies based on individual body shape and health characteristics, and its effectiveness will be evaluated through a 12-month randomized controlled trial among adults with obesity.

YEAR 1 · 2026

Precision Anthropometric Data and Body Shape Phenotyping

Establish a standardized three-dimensional whole-body scanning system and construct a precision anthropometric dataset of adults with obesity. Apply AI-based clustering to define body shape types and develop novel quantitative and visual body shape indicators that complement conventional BMI-based classification. Prepare the study protocol and personalized intervention strategy for the subsequent randomized controlled trial.

YEAR 2 · 2027

Development of an AI-Based Personalized Intervention

Construct a multidimensional Deep Health Profile by integrating three-dimensional anthropometry, dietary intake, lifestyle factors, clinical biomarkers, genetic information, gut microbiome profiles, physical activity, and sleep data. Develop an AI-based personalized nutrition and health feedback system incorporating 3D body visualization and the Health Belief Model. Initiate a 12-month randomized controlled trial and complete the baseline and 6-month follow-up assessments.

YEAR 3 · 2028

Clinical Evaluation and System Validation

Complete the 12-month longitudinal follow-up and evaluate the effects of the personalized intervention on body fat percentage, body shape, metabolic indicators, and health-related behaviors. Identify longitudinal patterns of change in the newly developed body shape indicators and assess the clinical effectiveness and sustainability of the intervention. Finalize the personalized nutrition and health intervention model and feedback algorithm.
